# Mobile Health (mHealth) Technologies for Monitoring Environmental Exposures and Health Impacts in Low-Income Communities

##### Study Title: Mobile Health (mHealth) for Monitoring Environmental Exposures and Health Impacts in Low-Income Communities: A Community-Engaged Pilot Study

**PI: Christine Ekenga, School of Public Health, Environmental Health**

Low-income communities experience chronic exposure to multiple social and environmental stressors. There is growing interest in utilizing mobile health (mHealth) technologies to monitor and improve health in these low-income settings. In addition to providing real-time health and environmental data, mHealth technologies may be useful for actively engaging users in environmental health promotion. In collaboration with local community partners, this project will evaluate whether smartphone-based mobile monitoring is a feasible and acceptable means of collecting environmental, social, and health outcome data from residents of predominantly low-income communities. This pilot project will be a key first step in identifying implementation strategies for adopting mHealth technologies to identify and intervene in local environmental health concerns.

**How this study advances exposome science**: This project will serve as a proof-of-concept for future research and interventions that will utilize mHealth technologies for monitoring a variety of stressors and adverse health outcomes.

**Published:**  November 16, 2022  
**Back to:** [Community Engagement Activities](https://emoryhercules.com/category/community-engagement-activities/)

# Benefits of Mobile Phone Technology for Personal Environmental Monitoring

# Benefits of Mobile Phone Technology for Personal Environmental Monitoring

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

**Background:**
Tracking individuals in environmental epidemiological studies using novel mobile phone technologies can provide valuable information on geolocation and physical activity, which will improve our understanding of environmental exposures.

**Objective:**
The objective of this study was to assess the performance of one of the least expensive mobile phones on the market to track people's travel-activity pattern.

**Methods:**
Adults living and working in Barcelona (72/162 bicycle commuters) carried simultaneously a mobile phone and a Global Positioning System (GPS) tracker and filled in a travel-activity diary (TAD) for 1 week (N=162). The CalFit app for mobile phones was used to log participants' geographical location and physical activity. The geographical location data were assigned to different microenvironments (home, work or school, in transit, others) with a newly developed spatiotemporal map-matching algorithm. The tracking performance of the mobile phones was compared with that of the GPS trackers using chi-square test and Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test. The minute agreement across all microenvironments between the TAD and the algorithm was compared using the Gwet agreement coefficient (AC1).

**Results:**
The mobile phone acquired locations for 905 (29.2%) more trips reported in travel diaries than the GPS tracker (P<.001) and had a median accuracy of 25 m. Subjects spent on average 57.9%, 19.9%, 9.0%, and 13.2% of time at home, work, in transit, and other places, respectively, according to the TAD and 57.5%, 18.8%, 11.6%, and 12.1%, respectively, according to the map-matching algorithm. The overall minute agreement between both methods was high (AC1 .811, 95% CI .810-.812).

**Conclusions:**
The use of mobile phones running the CalFit app provides better information on which microenvironments people spend their time in than previous approaches based only on GPS trackers. The improvements of mobile phone technology in microenvironment determination are because the mobile phones are faster at identifying first locations and capable of getting location in challenging environments thanks to the combination of assisted-GPS technology and network positioning systems. Moreover, collecting location information from mobile phones, which are already carried by individuals, allows monitoring more people with a cheaper and less burdensome method than deploying GPS trackers.

**Keywords:**
automatic data processing; cell phones; environmental exposure; mobile applications; monitoring, ambulatory; smartphone; spatio-temporal analysis; travel.

©David Donaire-Gonzalez, Antònia Valentín, Audrey de Nazelle, Albert Ambros, Glòria Carrasco-Turigas, Edmund Seto, Michael Jerrett, Mark J Nieuwenhuijsen. Originally published in JMIR Mhealth and Uhealth (http://mhealth.jmir.org), 10.11.2016.

# What role does mobile technology play in environmental health and safety compliance?

![How can you analyze audit trail data for insights into EHS performance?](https://sbnsoftware.com/wp-content/uploads/PastedGraphic-1-930x279.png)

As society becomes increasingly aware of environmental issues and the health consequences of industrial activities, the intersection of mobile technology and environmental health and safety (EHS) compliance has emerged as a critical area of focus. Mobile technology encompasses a range of devices and applications that facilitate communication, data collection, and analysis on-the-go. In the context of EHS compliance, this technological transformation is reshaping how organizations monitor, report, and respond to environmental and occupational health challenges. The integration of mobile solutions into EHS frameworks not only enhances compliance with regulations but also drives proactive management of risks associated with environmental and workplace hazards.

The ramifications of effective EHS compliance extend beyond mere legal adherence; they influence organizational reputation, employee well-being, and environmental sustainability. Mobile technology empowers organizations to streamline their compliance processes, enabling real-time data collection and instant reporting of safety incidents or environmental breaches. Mobile applications can facilitate training, audits, and inspections, ensuring that employees are informed and prepared to meet safety standards. Additionally, geo-location tools and sensors can enhance monitoring of environmental conditions, enabling swift responses to potential hazards. As such, mobile technology is not just a tool for efficiency but also a vital contributor to a culture of safety and environmental stewardship within organizations.

Moreover, the capabilities of mobile technology foster enhanced collaboration and communication among stakeholders, including regulatory bodies, employees, and the community. Through mobile platforms, organizations can disseminate information swiftly and accurately, fostering transparency and accountability in EHS practices. The ability to capture user-generated data and feedback in real-time amplifies the effectiveness of EHS programs, allowing organizations to identify trends, mitigate risks, and implement corrective actions swiftly. In essence, the adoption of mobile technology in EHS compliance represents a paradigm shift, moving from reactive approaches to proactive strategies that prioritize health and safety while minimizing environmental impact. As we delve deeper into this pivotal subject, it becomes increasingly evident that the role of mobile technology in EHS compliance is not only significant but fundamental in navigating the complexities of contemporary environmental and safety challenges.

## Mobile Data Collection and Reporting

Mobile data collection and reporting represent a significant advancement in how organizations manage environmental health and safety (EHS) compliance. By leveraging mobile technology, companies can gather and input data on-site in real time, rather than relying on manual processes that are often time-consuming and prone to error. Mobile platforms enable employees to capture data via smartphones or tablets directly in the field, whether it’s conducting inspections, monitoring pollution levels, or documenting safety incidents. This immediacy allows for a more accurate and comprehensive view of an organization’s EHS status.

The benefits of mobile data collection extend beyond mere convenience; they also enhance the quality and speed of reporting. Employees can easily upload photos, videos, and detailed notes alongside quantitative measurements, creating a rich data set that provides context and clarity. This holistic view can aid in identifying trends, facilitating risk assessments, and complying with regulatory requirements. Additionally, automated data entry reduces the risk of transcription errors and helps ensure that the information collected is both reliable and actionable.

Furthermore, mobile reporting tools often come with integrated analytics capabilities. This feature can help organizations visualize their compliance status through dashboards, making it easier to communicate findings to management and stakeholders. Access to real-time data can empower leaders to make informed decisions more swiftly, enabling proactive management of environmental health and safety issues. Additionally, having consistent data collection practices fosters a culture of accountability and awareness among employees, encouraging them to engage with EHS initiatives actively.

In conclusion, mobile data collection and reporting are vital components of a robust EHS compliance strategy. Their ability to streamline data gathering processes while providing real-time insights significantly enhances an organization’s capacity to manage and mitigate environmental health and safety risks effectively. By harnessing the power of mobile technology, organizations not only comply with regulations but also promote a safer and more sustainable working environment.

## Real-Time Monitoring and Alerts

Real-time monitoring and alerts are critical components in the realm of environmental health and safety (EHS) compliance. Utilizing mobile technology for real-time monitoring allows organizations to track various environmental parameters and safety metrics continuously, offering immediate insight into operational conditions. This capability is particularly valuable in industries where environmental hazards can change rapidly, such as manufacturing, construction, and chemical processing. By employing sensors and devices that transmit data to mobile platforms, organizations can ensure that they are aware of any deviations from safety standards as they occur, allowing for swift action to mitigate risks.

The role of mobile technology in EHS compliance extends beyond just monitoring; it also enables timely alerts to be sent to relevant personnel. For instance, if a sensor detects an unsafe level of a hazardous substance in the air or an anomalous temperature fluctuation in a critical system, a mobile alert can notify safety officers or facility managers immediately. This instant communication can prevent potential accidents or health risks by enabling timely intervention. Moreover, customizable alert systems can help different teams prioritize their responses based on the severity of the situation, ensuring that the most critical issues are addressed first.

Furthermore, the integration of mobile technology into real-time monitoring systems improves data collection and analysis. Mobile devices can be used to visualize data trends, making it easier for organizations to recognize patterns that could indicate emerging safety hazards or environmental concerns. The ability to efficiently gather and analyze this data is pivotal in enhancing decision-making processes related to EHS compliance. By leveraging insights gained through mobile monitoring, organizations can refine their operational strategies, align with regulatory requirements, and ultimately foster a safer and healthier working environment.

In summary, mobile technology significantly enhances environmental health and safety compliance through its real-time monitoring and alert capabilities. By facilitating immediate access to critical data and timely communications, organizations can proactively manage safety risks and maintain compliance with environmental regulations. The integration of real-time monitoring into EHS processes not only safeguards employee well-being but also supports the broader goal of sustainable operational practices.

## Mobile Training and Education Tools

Mobile training and education tools have significantly transformed how organizations address environmental health and safety compliance. By leveraging mobile technology, companies can provide their employees with immediate access to training resources, guidelines, and updates related to compliance requirements. This advancement is particularly vital in dynamic work environments where regulations may frequently change, and where immediate knowledge is crucial for ensuring safety and compliance.

One of the primary advantages of mobile training tools is their accessibility. Employees can engage with training materials, videos, and interactive content directly from their smartphones or tablets at any time and from any location. This flexibility enables training to occur on the job, minimizing disruptions and making it easier to integrate learning into daily routines. For example, safety protocols can be reviewed just before entering a potentially hazardous area, ensuring that employees are well-informed and prepared to manage any risks effectively.

Moreover, mobile tools can facilitate continuous education and refresher courses. Rather than relying solely on traditional classroom settings, companies can utilize apps and online platforms to deliver short, focused training sessions tailored to specific roles or compliance issues. This adaptability helps to maintain high levels of awareness and preparedness among workers, particularly in industries where regulations are subject to frequent updates or where new technologies and processes are consistently introduced.

Mobile training and education also enable tracking and assessment of employee engagement and comprehension. Organizations can incorporate quizzes and assessments within mobile platforms to evaluate understanding and retention of material essential for compliance. Furthermore, managers can monitor progress and completion rates, identifying employees who may need additional support. As a result, mobile training tools not only improve compliance but also foster a culture of safety and responsibility among employees.

In summary, mobile technology plays a crucial role in enhancing environmental health and safety compliance through innovative training and education tools. By making learning resources readily available and engaging, organizations can better equip their workforce to adhere to regulations, respond effectively to safety challenges, and create a safer workplace overall.

## Compliance Tracking and Management

Compliance tracking and management is a critical component of ensuring environmental health and safety (EHS) regulations are met effectively and efficiently. This process involves maintaining a systematic approach to tracking compliance with various environmental laws, safety standards, and internal policies. Mobile technology plays a vital role in this area, facilitating real-time access to compliance data, streamlining documentation processes, and improving overall transparency within organizations.

With mobile applications designed for compliance management, staff can easily input and access information related to compliance requirements while on-site. This immediacy is particularly beneficial for organizations that operate in dynamic environments where adherence to regulations must be verified frequently and without delay. For instance, mobile solutions can assist in tracking inspections, audits, and corrective actions, ensuring that employees can report findings directly from the field. This real-time functionality not only enhances the accuracy of collected data but also allows for rapid follow-up on any issues that may arise, thereby minimizing risks associated with non-compliance.

Moreover, mobile technology facilitates the automation of compliance reporting. Traditional methods often rely on extensive paperwork and manual entry, which is prone to human error and can be time-consuming. However, mobile compliance management tools can simplify this process by enabling automatic uploads of compliance data to centralized systems. This ensures that records are up-to-date and readily accessible for audits or regulatory review. Furthermore, data analytics derived from compliance tracking can be analyzed for patterns or recurring issues, offering organizations insight into potential areas of improvement.

In addition to tracking compliance but also serves a training and awareness role within the organization. Effective compliance management requires ongoing education about standards and regulatory changes. Mobile training tools can deliver timely updates and training modules to employees, ensuring they are informed of best practices and legal obligations. This informed workforce is vital for fostering a culture of safety and compliance, ultimately contributing to an organization’s environmental health performance. In summary, mobile technology enhances compliance tracking and management by improving data accuracy, streamlining processes, facilitating real-time decision-making, and supporting employee education.

![Simple But Needed blog banner image](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%20210%20140%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

## Geographic Information Systems (GIS) Integration

Geographic Information Systems (GIS) integration plays a pivotal role in environmental health and safety (EHS) compliance by providing spatial insights that are crucial for decision-making processes. GIS technology allows organizations to collect, manage, analyze, and visualize geographic data in relation to environmental factors. This capability enhances understanding of how different environmental elements—such as pollutants, hazardous waste sites, and natural resources—are distributed in a spatial context. By mapping these elements, organizations can identify patterns, assess risks, and make informed decisions that promote safety and sustainability.

One of the main benefits of GIS integration in EHS contexts is its ability to streamline data analysis and reporting. EHS professionals can use GIS to visualize data on contamination sites, air quality indices, or noise pollution levels across different geographic areas. This visualization allows for easier identification of areas that may require immediate attention or further investigation. Moreover, it assists in planning appropriate interventions, whether they involve regulatory compliance activities, community engagement, or strategic environmental management initiatives. The use of spatial data can significantly enhance efforts in ensuring compliance with environmental laws and regulations, as well as contributing to public health and safety.

Furthermore, mobile technology enhances the utilization of GIS by allowing for field data collection and analysis on-the-go. Workers equipped with mobile devices can capture real-time location-based data, which is automatically integrated into GIS platforms. This integration supports rapid response to emerging environmental issues, as data can be collected and analyzed in real time. For instance, if a hazardous material spill occurs, mobile GIS applications enable quick mapping of the affected area, assessment of potential risks to human health, and coordination of response efforts to mitigate the impact. In addition, mobile GIS tools facilitate communication and sharing of information among team members, allowing for better collaboration and faster decision-making in compliance efforts.

In summary, the integration of GIS within mobile technology frameworks significantly enhances environmental health and safety compliance initiatives. By facilitating spatial analysis and real-time data collection, organizations can better manage environmental risks, comply with regulatory standards, and ultimately safeguard public health and the environment. The combination of GIS and mobile tools represents a forward-thinking approach that aligns with the needs of modern EHS professionals, enabling them to work efficiently and effectively in a dynamic and data-driven landscape.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Mobile health (mHealth) technologies enable real-time monitoring of environmental exposures, actively engaging communities in health promotion while streamlining data collection for better intervention strategies.
Remote sensing utilizes satellite imagery and aerial data to monitor environmental changes, allowing for the tracking of pollution levels and land use patterns over large geographic areas.

Crowdsourcing leverages community participation to gather diverse data on environmental health issues, enhancing surveillance efforts by incorporating local knowledge and real-time reporting from residents.

Wastewater-based epidemiology analyzes sewage samples to detect pathogens and chemical contaminants, providing valuable insights into public health trends and environmental safety through community-level data.
